payload.text:
Refactor the entire frontend codebase to follow enterprise-grade MNC React architecture.

CORE REQUIREMENTS (MANDATORY)

Everything must be inside the src/ folder
No loose files outside src.

Folder-based modular architecture
Every feature/component must have its own individual folder (like big MNC companies).

Routing must be centralized and clean

Use react-router-dom

Central router file

Lazy loading where possible

Protected routes support

Complete redesign of structure + code

Clean, scalable, maintainable

Production-ready

No messy imports

No flat structure


src/
│
├── app/
│   ├── App.tsx
│   ├── AppRouter.tsx
│   └── Providers.tsx
│
├── routes/
│   └── routes.tsx
│
├── layouts/
│   ├── MainLayout/
│   │   ├── MainLayout.tsx
│   │   └── index.ts
│   └── AuthLayout/
│       ├── AuthLayout.tsx
│       └── index.ts
│
├── components/
│   ├── Header/
│   │   ├── Header.tsx
│   │   ├── Header.styles.ts
│   │   └── index.ts
│   │
│   ├── Sidebar/
│   │   ├── Sidebar.tsx
│   │   └── index.ts
│   │
│   ├── Chat/
│   │   ├── Chat.tsx
│   │   ├── ChatItem.tsx
│   │   └── index.ts
│
├── pages/
│   ├── Dashboard/
│   │   ├── Dashboard.tsx
│   │   ├── Dashboard.hooks.ts
│   │   └── index.ts
│   │
│   ├── Login/
│   │   ├── Login.tsx
│   │   └── index.ts
│
├── features/
│   ├── auth/
│   │   ├── auth.api.ts
│   │   ├── auth.slice.ts
│   │   └── auth.types.ts
│   │
│   ├── chat/
│   │   ├── chat.api.ts
│   │   ├── chat.slice.ts
│   │   └── chat.types.ts
│
├── hooks/
│   ├── useAuth.ts
│   └── useDebounce.ts
│
├── services/
│   ├── apiClient.ts
│   └── firebase.ts
│
├── store/
│   ├── index.ts
│   └── rootReducer.ts
│
├── utils/
│   ├── constants.ts
│   └── helpers.ts
│
├── styles/
│   ├── global.css
│   └── theme.ts
│
├── assets/
│   ├── images/
│   └── icons/
│
└── main.tsx

Each folder must expose via index.ts

No direct deep imports

Clean naming conventions

Feature-based separation

Components must be dumb

Business logic must be in hooks / features

Reusable UI only in components

Pages only handle layout + composition

ROUTING REQUIREMENTS

Use AppRouter.tsx

Support:

Public routes

Protected routes

Layout-based routing

Example:

/login → AuthLayout

/dashboard → MainLayout

Do NOT keep old messy structure

Do NOT put everything in one folder

Do NOT skip index.ts files

Do NOT ignore router abstraction
he final codebase should look like it was built by:

A senior frontend team at a global MNC

Refactor all existing files, move them into this structure, update imports, routing

**Envisioning Refactor Goals**

I'm now contemplating how to strategically restructure the frontend codebase. The goal is a complete overhaul, migrating to a robust, enterprise-ready React architecture akin to those used by MNCs. Currently, I'm focusing on organizing the logic into a new, nested folder structure.


**Planning Routing Strategy**

I'm now zeroing in on implementing proper routing with `react-router-dom`. My current focus is the existing flat structure. I'm aiming to replace the state-based "routing" in `App.tsx` with a cleaner, more robust `react-router-dom` implementation.  I will be adding it to the import map.


**Defining App Structure**

I've been busy creating the new file structure. I've moved `main.tsx`, and set up the app component. I've started building the layouts for authentication and the main app and planning the page organization. I'm considering where to put the chat logic, and I'll need to move some services and constants to their new homes. The goal is a clean, organized, scalable structure.


**Updating Routes & Imports**

I'm currently finalizing the routing structure by mapping each existing view to a new URL path, resolving how to handle the dynamic `blog-detail` route. Next, I'll be updating all the import statements to reflect the new file structure. This will ensure everything's properly linked within the refactored project. I've also identified components like `Navbar` and `Footer` that need navigation updates. The plan is to replace any `setView` calls with `Link` or `useNavigate`. I'm going to also check how to handle state in `PropertyCard`. As a final note, I plan to move the data used in `App.tsx`, such as `properties`, to a context, or simply directly import them to the pages.


**Managing Property Details**

I'm now focusing on the `PropertyDetailModal`. I'm considering integrating this within a `UIContext` or a dedicated `ModalContext` to manage its state globally, particularly if it should overlay other components. Alternatively, I'll place the modal directly within the `MainLayout` and control its visibility using a custom hook or context. I also am evaluating if it needs to be a route. In terms of implementation, I plan to begin by updating `index.html` to add `react-router-dom` to the import map, followed by creating `src/main.tsx` as the entry point. Next, I will be setting up `App.tsx` and `AppRouter.tsx`, and subsequently, the layouts: `MainLayout` and `AuthLayout`. I'll also start moving and refactoring relevant components.


**Implementing Components & Services**

I'm now prioritizing the relocation of services and constants, specifically focusing on `constants.ts` and `gemini.service.ts`, ensuring they're correctly integrated within the project's new architecture. I'm also preparing to refactor `Navbar` to utilize `Link` elements. My immediate goal is to establish a solid foundation for the pages and their dependencies, paving the way for a smooth transition and enhanced maintainability. Next, I'll concentrate on implementing a dedicated `PropertyModalContext` to manage the visibility and data for the `PropertyDetailModal`. This setup will allow for global control of the modal.


**Building Component Structure**

I'm now focusing on integrating the various components and pages within the new folder structure. Specifically, I'm working on moving and organizing the following files: `MainLayout.tsx`, `AuthLayout.tsx`, `ModalContext.tsx`, `Button.tsx`, `Navbar.tsx`, `Footer.tsx`, `PropertyCard.tsx`, `PropertyDetailModal.tsx`, `AcrelyAIChat.tsx`, `Snowfall.tsx`, `SettingsModal.tsx`, and the pages `Home.tsx` through `AuthPage.tsx`. My current focus is establishing a streamlined and efficient component organization that promotes code reusability and maintainability. My first priority is updating the `index.html` file to properly incorporate `react-router-dom`, followed by constructing the new file structure.


**Integrating Router & State**

I'm now integrating `react-router-dom` and planning where to place the existing state management. I've decided to move `isSnowEnabled`, `isDarkMode`, and potentially the modal states into a `ThemeContext` and `ModalContext`, respectively. The `scrolled` state will likely reside in the `Navbar` or `MainLayout`. I'm starting by updating `index.html` with `react-router-dom` and refactoring `App.tsx` accordingly to use these new contexts. I will add the necessary import maps to the import map.


**Planning Implementation Details**

I'm now outlining a detailed implementation plan, focusing on directory structure. I'm starting with `index.html` to integrate React Router. I'll then set up `src/main.tsx` as the root, followed by essential type definitions and utility constants.  I'll be building out services, contexts for themes and modals, and the various components including layouts, app routes, and page components.  Finally, I will handle the feature components, starting with AcrelyAIChat. I intend to build the components within their respective directories.


**Mapping Directory Structure**

I'm now carefully mapping out the directory structure. I've decided to move the `Button.tsx` to `common/Button.tsx`. I am moving the listed component files to logical folders as well, so that the structure is as organized as possible. I'll include components and pages.


**Refining File Organization**

I'm now revising my file list based on the architectural directives. The core is the `src` folder, so I'm moving `main.tsx` into it and updating `index.html` accordingly. I'm also planning the `app` folder, containing core app components.  I will be making folders for the `types`, `utils`, `services` and `context` as required. Finally, I will be planning the `layouts` and `common` folder. The priority is to satisfy the "inside src" rule while avoiding unnecessary nesting.


**Finalizing File Placement**

I'm currently finalizing the exact placement of all components and pages within the new directory structure. I'm carefully reviewing the revised file list, making sure everything is in its most logical location to ensure a clean, maintainable project. I've finished the initial organization and am preparing to implement these changes, ensuring there are no orphaned files.  I am confident that the structure is solid and well-organized now.


**Confirming Directory Logic**

I'm now clarifying the directory structure implementation, specifically regarding the "src/" folder. My focus is on ensuring consistency with the rule that everything must be inside "src/", yet that the project root is the current directory. To resolve this, I'm verifying the interpretation: the absence of `src/` prefixes in file paths implies that the project root is conceptually "src/", while a physical "src/" folder is assumed to exist. With this in mind, I will proceed with creating the necessary files inside the implied `src` directory without prepending "src/" to the file paths in the XML output.


**Organizing Core Files**

I'm now starting with the file structure. I'm focusing on the entry point and core files, placing them within the `src/` directory. I'm adding `react-router-dom` to `index.html`'s import map, updating the entry point to `src/main.tsx`. Next, I'm copying `types.ts` to `src/types/index.ts`, `constants.ts` to `src/utils/constants.ts`, and moving `gemini.service.ts` to `src/services/geminiService.ts`. I will move the remaining service code files there as well.


**Extending Service Integration**

I'm now integrating services like `geminiService.ts` within the new directory structure, specifically under `src/services/`. I'm starting by moving the file into the `services` directory and updating its import statements. I'm also ensuring that these services are correctly wired with the UI components that utilize them. This move is part of the larger refactor to organize the codebase. I will be doing this for all service related files.


**Building Project Structure**

I'm now focusing on arranging and building out the remaining components within the refined directory structure. I'm prioritizing the layout components like `Navbar` and `Footer`, then moving on to features such as `AcrelyAIChat` and `SettingsModal`, and then the property components. After which, the layouts, routers and pages will be built out. The objective is to establish a solid foundation for pages to load. I will check for un-used imports as I work.


**Revising Import Logic**

I'm now revising the import logic for `react-router-dom` to correctly use the ESM modules from `esm.sh` in `AppRouter` and other components. I am ensuring that the import statements for `Link`, `NavLink`, `useNavigate`, and `Outlet` are accurate and pointing to the correct ESM module. I'm also updating the `BlogDetail` component to retrieve the post data using the ID from the constants, rather than via object passing.

Scaling a startup in the real estate technology sector requires a blend of innovation, market understanding, and operational excellence. As we've grown Acrely, we've learned that sustainable growth isn't just about speed—it's about building a solid foundation.

Product-Market Fit is Key

Before aggressive scaling, ensure your solution truly solves a pain point. In PropTech, this means addressing trust deficits and process inefficiencies.

The Trust Deficit

The market is flooded with new ventures. Distinguishing legitimate, high-potential teams from fly-by-night operators is a challenge. Verified profiles help bridge this gap.

Transparency Wins

Openly sharing backgrounds, past achievements, and even failures creates a culture of transparency. This attracts better talent, smarter capital, and loyal customers.

payload.text: Delete all duplicate root-level folders. Keep ONLY index.html and src/. Move every component, page, service, and constant strictly inside src/. Refactor Navbar/Footer into layouts/MainLayout. Add index.ts barrel files everywhere. Separate business logic into features/. Update all imports and router paths accordingly.

**Refining Project Architecture**

I'm currently dissecting the existing project structure. Right now, it looks like this: root files (`index.tsx`, `App.tsx`, `types.ts`, `constants.ts`) and folders like `pages/`, `components/`, `services/`. This provides a baseline. Next I will consider a move to a more feature-based approach for organization.


**Structuring into `src/`**

I'm now focusing on moving everything into the `src/` directory. The goal is to streamline the root level by keeping only `index.html` and `src/`.  I'm structuring within `src/` into components, pages, layouts, and features. Features will house components related to specific business logic or functionalities.  I am thinking this separation will lead to better organization.


**Detailing New Folder Structure**

I've been meticulously defining the specific folder structure within `src/`.  I am now creating `src/components/features/` which will house logic specific components. Other folders will house API services, types, constants, helpers, and context providers. My current focus is on the app entry and routing within `src/app/`, and refining the layout hierarchy. I believe this structure will make maintaining the project much simpler.


**Implementing Component Relocation**

I'm currently busy migrating existing components and pages into the newly defined directory structure. I've just moved `Footer.tsx` to `/components/layout/`, and I've relocated a bunch of other components and pages into their respective designated folders. My aim now is to ensure all files are in their correct final resting spots, following the new architecture.


**Confirming Relocations**

I've just finished relocating all the files! Specifically, I moved `Contact.tsx` and `AuthPage.tsx` into their respective `pages/` folders. Also, `index.ts` from types, `constants.ts` from utils, `geminiService.ts` from services, and both `ThemeContext.tsx` and `ModalContext.tsx` from the context directory have been correctly positioned within `src/`. All existing components and pages are now residing within the appropriate folders of the redesigned project structure.


**Defining Application Structure**

I'm now focusing on the final file structure and rewrites.  I'm making a new `src/app/` folder, rewriting `main.tsx` for React 18 standards, and creating `AppRouter.tsx` along with `Providers.tsx`.  I'll ensure layouts and existing components and pages are correctly positioned. The `ThemeContext` and `ModalContext` will be integrated.


**Organizing Layout Components**

I'm currently finalizing the layout components. I've defined `MainLayout` with `Navbar`, `Footer`, and global overlays, considering moving overlay state to context for better management. Also, I've created `AuthLayout` for login/signup wrappers, and confirmed the directory structure for types, constants, and services, and ensured all components have adjusted imports and correct positioning for use.


**Updating React-Router**

I'm currently replacing the `view` state with `react-router-dom` for navigation, a significant update. This involves updating imports, especially within the pages and components. My immediate focus is on updating the `index.html` to include `react-router-dom` in the importmap, paving the way for the routing overhaul. Once this is done, I will update app logic. Then, I can move to generating the XML data.


**Finalizing Router and Context**

I'm now focusing on wiring up `react-router-dom` in `src/main.tsx` and structuring the `App.tsx` file to contain `Providers` wrapping the `Router`. I'm also preparing to make the switch from `setView` to the `useNavigate` hook within components and pages. For the `ModalContext`, I'm going to keep it as a global overlay controlled by context within the `MainLayout` which should improve user experience, and have made relevant updates to the layout structure. The `Blog Detail` page will be a dynamic route.

**Creating Core Layout Components**

I'm presently building the `MainLayout`, `AuthLayout`, and related layout components within their designated folders, considering imports and component hierarchy. I'm focusing on ensuring these layout components are correctly structured and interact seamlessly with the existing pages and features. Additionally, I am ensuring all relative paths for components are correct.

To restructure the application into a scalable architecture, I have organized the codebase entirely within `src/`, implemented `react-router-dom` for navigation, centralized state management with Context, and refactored components into domain-specific folders.

Here are the changes:

1.  **Architecture**: Moved to a Feature-First folder structure inside `src/`.
2.  **Routing**: Replaced manual `view` state with `react-router-dom` (v6).
3.  **Layouts**: Created `MainLayout` (Navbar + Footer) and `AuthLayout` (Login/Signup).
4.  **Context**: Added `ThemeContext` (Settings/Snowfall) and `ModalContext` (Property Details).
5.  **Refactoring**: Separation of concerns—Pages are now just views, Layouts handle structure, Features handle business logic.

Scaling a startup in the real estate technology sector requires a blend of innovation, market understanding, and operational excellence. As we've grown Acrely, we've learned that sustainable growth isn't just about speed—it's about building a solid foundation.

Product-Market Fit is Key

Before aggressive scaling, ensure your solution truly solves a pain point. In PropTech, this means addressing trust deficits and process inefficiencies.

Technology as an Enabler

Leverage AI and data analytics not just as buzzwords, but as tools to provide tangible value to your users—whether it's accurate pricing models or seamless virtual tours.

`, author: "Sanjay Aaron", date: "Oct 24, 2024", readTime: "5 min read", image: "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1519389950473-47ba0277781c?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80&w=1000", category: "Growth" }, { id: 2, title: "Why Founder Verification Matters", excerpt: "Building trust in the ecosystem through transparency and vetted backgrounds. In the startup ecosystem, and particularly in high-stakes industries like real estate and fintech, trust is paramount. Founder verification isn't just a checkbox; it's a fundamental layer of security and credibility for investors and customers alike.

The Trust Deficit

The market is flooded with new ventures. Distinguishing legitimate, high-potential teams from fly-by-night operators is a challenge. Verified profiles help bridge this gap.

Transparency Wins

Openly sharing backgrounds, past achievements, and even failures creates a culture of transparency. This attracts better talent, smarter capital, and loyal customers.

At Acrely, we believe in radical transparency. Knowing the people behind the platform ensures accountability and fosters long-term relationships.

`, author: "Priya Sharma", date: "Nov 02, 2024", readTime: "4 min read", image: "https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1556761175-5973dc0f32e7?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80&w=1000", category: "Trust & Safety" }, { id: 3, title: "How to Find the Right Co-Founder", excerpt: "Key traits to look for when building your founding team in the property sector. Building a company is a marathon, not a sprint. Having the right co-founder by your side can be the difference between burning out and crossing the finish line. But how do you find 'the one'?

Complementary Skill Sets

If you're technical, look for someone with business acumen. If you're a visionary, find an operator. Overlap is good for communication, but divergence is essential for coverage.

Values Alignment

Skills can be hired, but values cannot. Ensure you share the same ethical framework and long-term vision for the company. Disagreements on strategy are healthy; disagreements on values are fatal.

Test the waters with a small project before committing. The pressure of a startup will amplify any cracks in the relationship, so choose wisely.
